title: Privacy & Data Protection Awareness
description: A comprehensive introduction to privacy and data protection principles under ISO 27701, aligned with GDPR requirements. This module equips staff with the foundational knowledge needed to handle personal data responsibly and in compliance with organisational privacy policies.

sections.title: Why Privacy Matters
sections.content:
Privacy is a fundamental right that underpins trust between organisations and the individuals whose data they process. In an era of increasing digitalisation, the volume of personal data collected, stored, and shared has grown exponentially, making robust privacy practices essential to maintaining public confidence and regulatory compliance.

Organisations that fail to protect personal data face significant consequences, including regulatory fines, reputational damage, and loss of customer trust. High-profile data breaches have demonstrated that the impact extends well beyond financial penalties, often resulting in lasting harm to brand perception and stakeholder relationships.

ISO 27701 provides a framework for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving a Privacy Information Management System (PIMS). By adopting this standard, organisations demonstrate a systematic and accountable approach to privacy that goes beyond mere compliance with individual regulations.

Every member of staff plays a critical role in protecting privacy. Whether you handle personal data directly or support those who do, understanding why privacy matters is the first step towards building a culture of data protection throughout the organisation.

sections.title: Key Data Protection Principles
sections.content:
Data protection principles form the backbone of any effective privacy programme. These principles, closely aligned with the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018, establish the standards against which all processing of personal data must be measured.

The core principles require that personal data be processed lawfully, fairly, and in a transparent manner. Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes, and must not be further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes. These requirements ensure that individuals understand how their data is being used and can exercise meaningful control over it.

Additional principles mandate that personal data be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary for the purposes of processing. Data must be kept accurate and up to date, and must not be retained for longer than is necessary. Appropriate technical and organisational measures must be in place to ensure the security of personal data.

The principle of accountability requires organisations to demonstrate compliance with all of the above principles. This means maintaining comprehensive records of processing activities, conducting regular reviews, and being prepared to evidence compliance to supervisory authorities upon request.

sections.title: Lawful Basis for Processing
sections.content:
Before any personal data is processed, the organisation must identify and document a valid lawful basis. There are six lawful bases available under the UK GDPR: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, and legitimate interests. Selecting the correct basis is essential, as it determines the rights available to data subjects and the obligations placed upon the organisation.

Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. It requires a clear affirmative action from the data subject and must be as easy to withdraw as it is to give. Contractual necessity applies where processing is required to fulfil a contract with the data subject or to take pre-contractual steps at their request.

Legitimate interests provides the most flexible lawful basis but requires a documented balancing test to ensure that the organisation's interests do not override the rights and freedoms of the data subject. Legal obligation and public task bases apply where processing is mandated by law or necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest.

It is important to determine the lawful basis before processing begins and to communicate it to data subjects through a privacy notice. Changing the lawful basis retrospectively is generally not permissible unless the new basis is compatible with the original purpose of processing.

sections.title: Data Subject Rights
sections.content:
Individuals whose personal data is processed by the organisation enjoy a range of rights designed to give them control over their information. These rights include the right of access, the right to rectification, the right to erasure, the right to restrict processing, the right to data portability, and the right to object to processing.

The right of access, commonly exercised through a Subject Access Request (SAR), entitles individuals to obtain confirmation that their data is being processed, a copy of that data, and supplementary information about how it is used. Organisations must respond to SARs without undue delay and within one calendar month of receipt.

The right to erasure, often referred to as the right to be forgotten, allows individuals to request the deletion of their personal data in certain circumstances, such as where the data is no longer necessary for its original purpose or where consent has been withdrawn. However, this right is not absolute and may be overridden by legal obligations to retain data.

All staff must be able to recognise when a data subject is exercising one of their rights and must know how to escalate such requests promptly to the appropriate team. Failure to respond to rights requests within the statutory timeframe can result in complaints to the supervisory authority and potential enforcement action.

sections.title: PII Controller and Processor Obligations
sections.content:
ISO 27701 distinguishes between PII controllers and PII processors, each of which carries specific obligations. A PII controller determines the purposes and means of processing personal data, whilst a PII processor processes personal data on behalf of, and under the instructions of, the controller.

PII controllers bear primary responsibility for ensuring that processing is lawful, fair, and transparent. They must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, conduct data protection impact assessments where required, and ensure that any processors they engage provide sufficient guarantees of compliance. Controllers must also maintain records of processing activities and cooperate with supervisory authorities.

PII processors must process personal data only in accordance with the documented instructions of the controller. They are required to implement appropriate security measures, assist the controller in fulfilling data subject rights requests, and notify the controller without undue delay upon becoming aware of a personal data breach. Processors must also obtain prior authorisation before engaging sub-processors.

The relationship between controller and processor must be governed by a written contract or binding agreement that sets out the subject matter, duration, nature, and purpose of processing, as well as the obligations and rights of each party. Regular audits and reviews of processor compliance should form part of the organisation's ongoing assurance activities.

sections.title: Privacy by Design and Default
sections.content:
Privacy by design is a proactive approach that embeds data protection considerations into the design of systems, processes, and products from the outset, rather than treating privacy as an afterthought. This approach is both a regulatory requirement under the UK GDPR and a core principle of ISO 27701.

In practice, privacy by design means conducting privacy impact assessments during the planning phase of new projects, selecting technologies that minimise data collection, and implementing access controls that restrict personal data to those who genuinely need it. Default settings should be configured to provide the highest level of privacy protection, requiring users to opt in rather than opt out of data sharing.

Privacy by default extends this principle to ensure that, without any intervention from the data subject, only personal data that is necessary for each specific purpose is processed. This includes limiting the amount of data collected, the extent of processing, the period of storage, and the accessibility of the data.

Embedding privacy into organisational culture requires ongoing training, clear policies, and leadership commitment. Every new initiative, system change, or procurement decision should include a privacy review to ensure that data protection obligations are met from the very earliest stages of development.

title: PII Handling & Processing
description: This module provides practical guidance on the proper handling and processing of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) throughout its lifecycle. Staff will learn how to identify PII, apply data minimisation principles, and follow organisational procedures for retention and secure disposal.

sections.title: Identifying Personally Identifiable Information
sections.content:
Personally Identifiable Information (PII) is any information that can be used, either alone or in combination with other data, to identify a specific individual. Common examples include names, email addresses, telephone numbers, national insurance numbers, and financial details. However, PII extends well beyond these obvious identifiers.

Indirect identifiers such as IP addresses, device identifiers, location data, and online behavioural profiles can also constitute PII when they can be linked back to a specific individual. Even data that appears anonymised may qualify as PII if re-identification is reasonably possible using available means.

Special category data, sometimes referred to as sensitive personal data, requires additional safeguards. This includes information relating to racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership, genetic and biometric data, health information, and data concerning an individual's sex life or sexual orientation.

Staff must develop the ability to recognise PII in all its forms across the systems and processes they interact with. If there is any doubt as to whether information constitutes PII, it should be treated as such and handled in accordance with the organisation's data protection policies.

sections.title: Collection Limitation and Purpose Specification
sections.content:
The principle of collection limitation requires that personal data be obtained only through lawful and fair means, and where appropriate, with the knowledge or consent of the data subject. Organisations must not collect personal data speculatively or on the basis that it might prove useful in the future.

Purpose specification demands that the purposes for which personal data is collected be identified at or before the time of collection. These purposes must be documented clearly in privacy notices and internal records of processing activities. Any subsequent use of the data must be compatible with the original stated purpose.

When designing data collection processes, staff should critically evaluate whether each data element is genuinely required. Forms, surveys, and system inputs should be reviewed regularly to remove fields that collect unnecessary information. Where optional data is requested, this must be clearly distinguished from mandatory fields.

Purpose limitation also restricts the sharing of personal data with third parties. Data may only be disclosed to external recipients where this is consistent with the original purpose of collection, or where a separate lawful basis for the disclosure has been identified and documented.

sections.title: Data Minimisation
sections.content:
Data minimisation is the principle that personal data processed must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which it is processed. This principle is central to both ISO 27701 and the UK GDPR, and it serves as a practical safeguard against excessive data collection.

Implementing data minimisation requires a disciplined approach at every stage of the data lifecycle. During collection, only the minimum data required to achieve the specified purpose should be gathered. During processing, access to personal data should be restricted to those individuals who require it for their role, following the principle of least privilege.

Techniques such as pseudonymisation and aggregation can support data minimisation by reducing the identifiability of personal data whilst still enabling legitimate processing activities. Where analytical or statistical purposes can be achieved using anonymised data, this approach should be preferred over the use of identifiable personal data.

Regular data audits should be conducted to identify instances where more data is being collected or retained than is necessary. These audits provide an opportunity to streamline processes, reduce storage costs, and lower the organisation's overall risk exposure in the event of a data breach.

sections.title: Accuracy and Data Quality
sections.content:
The accuracy principle requires that personal data be kept accurate and, where necessary, up to date. Inaccurate data must be corrected or erased without undue delay. Maintaining data quality is not merely a regulatory obligation; it is essential for effective decision-making and for upholding the rights of data subjects.

Organisations should implement processes that enable data subjects to review and update their own information where practicable. Self-service portals, periodic verification prompts, and clear channels for reporting inaccuracies all contribute to maintaining high data quality standards.

Staff who enter, update, or migrate personal data bear a particular responsibility for accuracy. Data entry procedures should include validation checks, and bulk data operations should be subject to quality assurance reviews. Where personal data is received from third parties, its accuracy should be verified before it is incorporated into organisational systems.

Where data is found to be inaccurate, the organisation must take reasonable steps to rectify or erase it promptly. If inaccurate data has been shared with third parties, those recipients must be informed of the correction so that they can update their own records accordingly.

sections.title: Retention and Secure Disposal
sections.content:
Personal data must not be kept for longer than is necessary for the purposes for which it was collected. The organisation's data retention schedule defines the appropriate retention periods for different categories of personal data, taking into account legal, regulatory, and business requirements.

Staff should familiarise themselves with the retention schedule relevant to their area of work and ensure that data is reviewed and disposed of in accordance with the prescribed timelines. Automated retention controls should be implemented where possible to reduce the risk of data being retained beyond its approved period.

Secure disposal of personal data requires that data be rendered irrecoverable, regardless of the medium on which it is stored. Electronic data should be securely overwritten or cryptographically erased, whilst physical records containing personal data should be cross-cut shredded or incinerated by an approved disposal provider.

Disposal activities must be documented, including the date of destruction, the method used, and confirmation of completion. Where third-party disposal services are engaged, the organisation should obtain certificates of destruction as evidence of compliance. Regular audits of disposal processes help to ensure that retention policies are being followed consistently across the organisation.

title: Data Protection Impact Assessment
description: This module covers the purpose, methodology, and practical execution of Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) as required by ISO 27701 and the UK GDPR. Participants will learn when a DPIA is mandatory, how to conduct one effectively, and how to document and act upon the findings.

sections.title: When a DPIA Is Required
sections.content:
A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is a structured process for identifying and mitigating the data protection risks associated with a processing activity. Under the UK GDPR, a DPIA is mandatory where processing is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals.

Specific circumstances that trigger a mandatory DPIA include systematic and extensive profiling with significant effects, large-scale processing of special category data, and systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas. The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) has also published a list of processing activities that require a DPIA, which organisations should consult regularly.

Even where a DPIA is not strictly mandatory, it is good practice to conduct one for any new processing activity that involves personal data. A DPIA provides a documented basis for decision-making and demonstrates the organisation's commitment to proactive risk management and accountability.

The obligation to carry out a DPIA rests with the data controller, although processors may be required to assist. DPIAs should be initiated at the earliest possible stage of project planning, before processing begins, to ensure that privacy risks are identified and addressed before they materialise.

sections.title: DPIA Methodology
sections.content:
A robust DPIA methodology follows a structured sequence of steps designed to systematically identify, assess, and address privacy risks. The process typically begins with a description of the proposed processing, including the nature, scope, context, and purposes of the activity, as well as the categories and volumes of personal data involved.

The next step is to assess the necessity and proportionality of the processing in relation to its stated purpose. This involves evaluating whether the processing is genuinely required, whether less intrusive alternatives exist, and whether the lawful basis and safeguards are appropriate and sufficient.

Risk assessment follows, in which the potential impacts on data subjects are identified and evaluated. Risks should be assessed in terms of both likelihood and severity, considering factors such as the sensitivity of the data, the vulnerability of the data subjects, and the potential for harm, including physical, material, and non-material damage.

Finally, the DPIA should identify measures to mitigate the risks identified, such as additional technical controls, changes to the processing design, or enhanced governance arrangements. The completed assessment should be reviewed and approved by the Data Protection Officer or an equivalent authority before processing commences.

sections.title: Risk Identification and Mitigation
sections.content:
Effective risk identification requires a thorough examination of every stage of the proposed processing activity, from data collection through to retention and disposal. Common privacy risks include unauthorised access to personal data, accidental disclosure, excessive data collection, inadequate consent mechanisms, and insufficient security measures.

Risks should be documented in a structured risk register that captures the nature of each risk, its potential impact on data subjects, the likelihood of occurrence, and the resulting risk rating. This register serves as a living document that is updated as the processing activity evolves or as new risks emerge.

Mitigation measures should be proportionate to the level of risk identified. Technical measures may include encryption, pseudonymisation, access controls, and automated data retention enforcement. Organisational measures may include staff training, updated policies and procedures, contractual safeguards with third parties, and enhanced oversight arrangements.

Where residual risks remain high despite the implementation of mitigation measures, the organisation must consult the ICO before proceeding with the processing. This prior consultation process allows the supervisory authority to provide guidance on whether the proposed processing can proceed and, if so, what additional safeguards may be required.

sections.title: Documentation and Consultation Requirements
sections.content:
Thorough documentation is essential to demonstrating compliance with the DPIA obligation. The completed DPIA record should include a systematic description of the processing operations, the purposes, the assessment of necessity and proportionality, the risk assessment, and the measures envisaged to address those risks.

The Data Protection Officer (DPO) must be consulted during the DPIA process. The DPO's role is to provide independent advice on the assessment, to verify that the methodology has been applied correctly, and to confirm that the proposed mitigation measures are adequate. The DPO's advice and the decisions taken in response should be documented within the DPIA record.

Where the processing involves external stakeholders, consideration should be given to seeking the views of data subjects or their representatives. This consultation can take the form of surveys, focus groups, or public engagement exercises, and should be proportionate to the scale and sensitivity of the processing activity.

Completed DPIAs should be reviewed at regular intervals and whenever there is a material change to the processing activity. Reviews ensure that the assessment remains current and that any new risks are identified and addressed promptly. All DPIAs and their subsequent reviews should be retained as part of the organisation's accountability records and made available to the supervisory authority upon request.
